content.text: Every transformer layer consists of two sublayers: the multi-head attention sublayer that routes information between token positions, and the
feed-forward network (FFN)

— a position-wise, two-layer fully-connected MLP applied independently to every token vector after attention. The FFN is where individual token representations are nonlinearly transformed, and where a large fraction of the model's factual knowledge is stored. Understanding it precisely is essential for working with transformer models at any level of depth.
What it is
The FFN sublayer applies the same two-layer MLP to every position in the sequence, independently. For a single token vector
, the computation is:
where
expands the representation to an intermediate dimension
is an element-wise nonlinearity, and
projects back to
. In the original 2017 transformer,
and
. Contemporary models replace ReLU with GELU or SiLU (Swish), and some use gated variants (SwiGLU, GeGLU) with a different expansion ratio.
The FFN is applied
position-wise
: the same weight matrices
are shared across all
token positions, but each token is processed with no reference to other tokens. Operationally, when processing a batch of shape
, the FFN can be seen as reshaping to
, applying the two linear layers, then reshaping back. The batch and position dimensions are treated identically.
Why it matters
In parameter count, the FFN typically dominates the attention sublayer. For a model with
and
, the two FFN weight matrices hold

parameters per layer. With 32 layers that is over 4B parameters from the FFN alone — more than the attention parameters in most decoder-only models. This makes the FFN the primary carrier of learned representations.
Mechanistic interpretability experiments by Geva et al. (2021, 2022) demonstrated that individual neurons in

fire for human-interpretable input patterns (e.g., "years ending in 0", "names of European capitals"), and that FFN layers can be understood as performing key-value memory lookups: the first layer detects patterns (keys) and the second layer emits the associated content (values). This framing is important for model editing, targeted fine-tuning, and understanding why scaling model width (increasing
) improves factual recall disproportionately relative to scaling depth.
For agent system builders, the FFN's position-wise nature has a direct throughput consequence: unlike attention, which has quadratic cost in sequence length, FFN compute scales linearly in
. Very long prompts (large retrieval contexts, long tool-call histories) are expensive primarily because of the
attention term, not the FFN. Understanding which sublayer dominates informs hardware choices and batching strategies for agentic workloads.
Benefits

Universal function approximation per token: the expand-nonlinear-contract structure can approximate any continuous function on the token's representation, enabling arbitrarily complex per-token transformations

Factual storage capacity: the large intermediate dimension d_ff gives each layer millions of independently activatable neurons that collectively store a vast learned knowledge base, scaling proportionally with model width
Linear scaling with sequence length: FFN compute is O(n) versus O(n^2) for attention, so the FFN remains tractable even at very long context lengths where attention becomes the bottleneck
Parallelism across positions: because tokens are processed independently, the entire sequence can be processed with a single batched matrix multiplication — no sequential dependency between positions
Shared weights across positions: the same W1 and W2 apply to every token position, keeping the FFN parameter count independent of context length with no position-specific overhead

Complementary to attention: attention performs cross-token routing and mixing; the FFN performs per-token computation on the mixed representation, making the two sublayers functionally distinct and mutually reinforcing

Interpretable neuron structure: individual neurons in the intermediate layer can be probed for semantic meanings, enabling targeted model editing via rank-one updates to W1 and W2 (e.g., the ROME method)
Trade-offs

Largest parameter consumer: for standard d_ff = 4 * d_model, each FFN layer holds 8 * d_model^2 parameters across two matrices, typically exceeding the 4 * d_model^2 of the four attention matrices — the FFN dominates memory in wide models

No cross-token interaction: the FFN cannot directly attend to or combine information from other positions; it operates only on the vector already assembled by attention, and cannot recover missed dependencies

Dead neuron risk with ReLU: a large fraction of neurons can permanently saturate to zero if learning rates or initialization are poorly tuned; GELU and SiLU mitigate this by providing non-zero gradients almost everywhere

Memory-bandwidth bottleneck at decode: during autoregressive inference with batch size 1, all of W1 and W2 must be transferred from HBM on every generation step, making weight loading the dominant cost per token

Fixed knowledge capacity: unlike the attention KV cache which grows with context, the FFN represents a fixed lookup table; the model cannot dynamically extend its knowledge after training without retraining or fine-tuning
Sensitive expansion ratio: halving d_ff has roughly the same quality impact as removing an entire layer, making FFN width a critical hyperparameter that is expensive to change post-training
How it works
Two-projection structure
The first linear layer
expands each
-dimensional token vector into the higher-dimensional intermediate space
. This expansion provides room for the nonlinearity to act selectively: only some of the
neurons activate for any given input. The second layer
contracts back to
. The intuition is an inverted bottleneck: expand to enable diverse feature detection, apply nonlinearity to gate which features matter, then recombine into the model's working dimension.
The Geva et al. key-value memory interpretation makes this precise: row
of
is a key pattern that detects a specific input feature; the corresponding column
of
is the value emitted when that key fires. The nonlinearity gates which keys activate, and the second layer sums the activated values. This is formally identical to a soft key-value memory lookup.
Activation functions
The original transformer used
. Modern models almost universally prefer smoother alternatives. GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit) is defined as:
SiLU (also called Swish) is

. Both are smooth and provide non-zero gradients for slightly negative inputs, significantly reducing dead neurons. Llama, Mistral, and most recent open-source models use SiLU in combination with a gated variant called SwiGLU.
Gated FFN variants: SwiGLU and GeGLU
SwiGLU, introduced in the PaLM paper, replaces the single first projection with two parallel projections whose outputs are element-wise multiplied:
Here
are two learned gate matrices. The element-wise product
acts as a learned gate: one projection controls what information flows, the other controls how strongly it flows. To maintain parameter count comparable to a standard FFN, SwiGLU models use
— the factor
compensates for the additional third projection matrix. Llama-2 and Llama-3 both use SwiGLU.
Residual connection and layer normalization
The FFN output is added back to the pre-FFN input via a residual (skip) connection:

This is the pre-norm formulation used by modern LLMs (LayerNorm applied before each sublayer input). The original transformer used post-norm (LayerNorm after addition). Pre-norm trains more stably at depth and is now the de facto standard for models beyond GPT-2. The residual connection ensures the FFN only needs to learn
incremental corrections
to the existing representation rather than reconstructing it from scratch, significantly accelerating convergence and enabling training of very deep networks.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the full data flow through one FFN sublayer: input passes through a LayerNorm, then the first linear projection expands it to
, the activation applies element-wise, the second projection contracts back to
, dropout is applied, and the result is added to the original input via the residual. The shape entering and exiting is always
— the FFN is shape-preserving.
When to use it
The FFN is a built-in component of every transformer layer and is not an optional choice for standard model usage. The practical decisions arise in the following scenarios:

Training a model from scratch: choose d_ff, the activation function, and whether to use a gated variant (SwiGLU/GeGLU) — these are among the highest-impact architectural hyperparameters affecting both quality and throughput

Applying parameter-efficient fine-tuning: LoRA and adapter methods frequently target W1 and W2 because the FFN holds the most parameters and the most factual knowledge, making it the highest-leverage adaptation target
Model editing (ROME, MEMIT): directly modify specific rows of W1/W2 to update factual associations without full retraining — enabled by the key-value memory structure of the FFN

Pruning and sparsification: the expand-then-contract structure makes the FFN amenable to structured pruning of entire neurons in the intermediate dimension, reducing inference cost without changing the model architecture
Mixture-of-Experts: replace the dense FFN with multiple expert FFNs, routing each token to a subset of experts — all major MoE LLMs (Mixtral, Switch Transformer) use this pattern
Diagnosing output errors: if a model hallucinates a specific fact, the error may originate in the FFN layers that store that association; neuron activation analysis can localize the source
The FFN is
not

a useful site for controlling cross-token interactions: any intervention requiring information mixing between positions must happen at the attention layer. Removing or bypassing the FFN is only appropriate for structured pruning experiments where quality degradation is expected and measured.
How to implement
The following NumPy implementation builds a standard FFN and a SwiGLU-gated FFN from first principles, making the projection dimensions and gating mechanism directly visible in code.

import numpy as np


def gelu(x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """GELU approximation (Hendrycks & Gimpel 2016)."""
    return 0.5 * x * (1 + np.tanh(np.sqrt(2 / np.pi) * (x + 0.044715 * x**3)))


def silu(x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """SiLU / Swish activation."""
    return x / (1 + np.exp(-x))


class FeedForwardNetwork:
    """Standard two-layer FFN (position-wise MLP) with GELU activation."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_ff: int, seed: int = 0) -> None:
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
        scale1 = np.sqrt(2.0 / (d_model + d_ff))
        scale2 = np.sqrt(2.0 / (d_ff + d_model))
        self.W1 = rng.normal(0, scale1, (d_ff, d_model))   # (d_ff, d_model)
        self.b1 = np.zeros(d_ff)
        self.W2 = rng.normal(0, scale2, (d_model, d_ff))   # (d_model, d_ff)
        self.b2 = np.zeros(d_model)

    def forward(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        x: (n, d_model) or (d_model,) for a single token
        Returns same shape as input.
        """
        h = gelu(x @ self.W1.T + self.b1)  # (n, d_ff)
        return h @ self.W2.T + self.b2      # (n, d_model)


class SwiGLUFeedForward:
    """Gated FFN with SwiGLU activation (Shazeer 2020).
    Uses two expansion matrices and one contraction matrix.
    d_ff_gate is typically (2/3) * (4 * d_model) to match param count.
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_ff_gate: int, seed: int = 0) -> None:
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
        s = np.sqrt(2.0 / (d_model + d_ff_gate))
        self.W  = rng.normal(0, s, (d_ff_gate, d_model))  # gate activator
        self.V  = rng.normal(0, s, (d_ff_gate, d_model))  # gate controller
        self.W2 = rng.normal(0, s, (d_model, d_ff_gate))  # contraction

    def forward(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        gate = silu(x @ self.W.T)       # (n, d_ff_gate)
        content = x @ self.V.T          # (n, d_ff_gate)
        return (gate * content) @ self.W2.T  # (n, d_model)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    n, d_model = 8, 512
    x = np.random.randn(n, d_model)

    ffn = FeedForwardNetwork(d_model=d_model, d_ff=d_model * 4)
    out = ffn.forward(x)
    print(f"Standard FFN output shape : {out.shape}")   # (8, 512)

    d_ff_gate = int(2 / 3 * 4 * d_model)
    swiglu = SwiGLUFeedForward(d_model=d_model, d_ff_gate=d_ff_gate)
    out2 = swiglu.forward(x)
    print(f"SwiGLU FFN output shape   : {out2.shape}")  # (8, 512)

    std_params = 2 * (d_model * 4 * d_model)
    swi_params = 3 * (d_ff_gate * d_model)
    print(f"Standard FFN params: {std_params:,}")
    print(f"SwiGLU FFN params  : {swi_params:,}")
Examples
PyTorch module with pre-norm and residual

This is the complete FFN sublayer as it appears inside a transformer block, including the pre-norm, residual connection, and dropout, with support for multiple activation functions and a SwiGLU variant.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class FFNSubLayer(nn.Module):
    """Transformer FFN sublayer: pre-norm + two-layer MLP + residual.
    Supports 'relu', 'gelu', and 'silu' (Swish) activations.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        d_model: int,
        d_ff: int,
        dropout: float = 0.1,
        activation: str = "gelu",
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.W1 = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=True)
        self.W2 = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model, bias=True)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        act_map = {
            "relu": F.relu,
            "gelu": F.gelu,
            "silu": F.silu,
        }
        if activation not in act_map:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown activation: {activation}")
        self._act = act_map[activation]

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """x: (B, n, d_model) -> (B, n, d_model)"""
        residual = x
        x = self.norm(x)           # pre-norm
        x = self._act(self.W1(x))  # expand + activate: (B, n, d_ff)
        x = self.drop(x)
        x = self.W2(x)             # contract: (B, n, d_model)
        return residual + x        # residual


class SwiGLUSubLayer(nn.Module):
    """FFN sublayer using SwiGLU gating (Llama/PaLM style)."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_ff: int, dropout: float = 0.0) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.W   = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
        self.V   = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
        self.W2  = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model, bias=False)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        residual = x
        x = self.norm(x)
        gate = F.silu(self.W(x))  # (B, n, d_ff)
        x = gate * self.V(x)      # element-wise gating
        x = self.drop(x)
        x = self.W2(x)            # (B, n, d_model)
        return residual + x


if __name__ == "__main__":
    B, n, d_model = 2, 16, 512
    x = torch.randn(B, n, d_model)

    layer = FFNSubLayer(d_model=d_model, d_ff=2048, activation="gelu")
    out = layer(x)
    print(out.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 16, 512])

    d_ff_gate = int(2 / 3 * 4 * d_model)
    swiglu = SwiGLUSubLayer(d_model=d_model, d_ff=d_ff_gate)
    out2 = swiglu(x)
    print(out2.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 16, 512])
Real-world example
Llama-3-8B uses
, 32 layers, SwiGLU activation, and
. Each SwiGLU layer has three weight matrices:
and
. Parameter count per FFN layer:
. Across 32 layers: approximately 5.6B parameters from the FFN alone — roughly 70% of the total 8B parameter count.

During inference with bfloat16 weights, each FFN layer requires loading roughly 352 MB from device memory per generation step at batch size 1. With 32 layers that is about 11 GB of weight data read per token — far exceeding the compute ceiling on consumer GPUs and making memory bandwidth the primary bottleneck. This is why serving quantized models (4-bit or 8-bit weights) dramatically increases throughput: reducing FFN weight size directly cuts bandwidth cost.
A practical example of targeted FFN editing: the ROME method (Meng et al. 2022) updates a single row of

in a specified transformer layer to change one factual association (e.g., "The Eiffel Tower is in Rome" to "...is in Paris") without touching other layers. The method computes the exact rank-one update to
using a closed-form formula derived from the key-value memory interpretation. This works because the FFN's columnar structure in
directly maps neuron activations to output token distributions.

# Inspecting FFN neuron activation patterns in a pre-trained model
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B"  # small enough for inspection
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
model.eval()


def extract_ffn_activations(text: str, layer_idx: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Return the intermediate FFN gate activations at layer_idx."""
    activations = {}

    def hook(module, input, output):
        activations["gate"] = output.detach()

    # Hook into the gate_proj (W in SwiGLU) of the specified layer
    handle = model.model.layers[layer_idx].mlp.gate_proj.register_forward_hook(hook)

    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        model(**inputs)
    handle.remove()

    return activations["gate"]   # shape: (1, seq_len, d_ff)


acts = extract_ffn_activations("The capital of France is Paris.", layer_idx=8)
print(f"Activation shape: {acts.shape}")
# Find neurons with highest mean absolute activation across the sequence
mean_act = acts[0].abs().mean(0)
top10 = mean_act.topk(10).indices.tolist()
print(f"Top-10 activated FFN neurons (layer 8): {top10}")

# Measure sparsity: fraction of neurons with near-zero activation
threshold = 0.01
sparsity = (acts[0].abs() < threshold).float().mean().item()
print(f"Activation sparsity (threshold={threshold}): {sparsity:.2%}")
Common mistakes

Applying LayerNorm after instead of before the FFN (post-norm): post-norm architectures can diverge at depth beyond 12 layers unless carefully tuned with extended warmup; use pre-norm as the default for new model designs
Forgetting the residual connection: without the skip path, deep stacks of FFN+attention layers suffer vanishing gradients; the residual is not optional and must be present for training to converge

Using bias=True in W1/W2 for Llama-style models: modern decoder-only models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen) drop biases from FFN projection matrices; biases add parameters that complicate weight tying and quantization without meaningful quality gains

Setting d_ff to a non-multiple of 256: CUDA matrix multiplication kernels are heavily optimized for dimensions that are multiples of 64 or 256; arbitrary d_ff values trigger slower unoptimized code paths

Applying dropout inside the FFN during inference: dropout must be disabled at inference time; model.eval() in PyTorch sets this automatically, but custom implementations often forget; FFN outputs randomly zeroed at test time degrade quality significantly

Confusing the expansion ratio between standard and SwiGLU: standard FFN uses d_ff = 4 * d_model; SwiGLU targets the same parameter count with d_ff_gate = (2/3) * 4 * d_model; using 4 * d_model for SwiGLU inflates parameter count by 50%

Not matching activation function to checkpoint: loading a GELU-trained checkpoint into a ReLU model produces garbage outputs; always verify the activation in the original model config before running inference
Best practices

Prefer SwiGLU or GeGLU for new model training: these gated variants consistently outperform ReLU and plain GELU on language modelling benchmarks at equivalent parameter count, with no compute overhead for the same d_ff
Set d_ff as a multiple of the tensor-parallel degree: when sharding the FFN across N GPUs, d_ff must be divisible by N to enable even column-parallel splits of W1 and W2 without padding

Use pre-norm (LayerNorm before sublayer input) for all models with depth greater than 6 layers: post-norm requires careful learning rate schedules to avoid divergence and is only well-studied for shallow models

Apply dropout to the FFN intermediate activations (after the activation function, before W2), not after W2: post-W2 dropout regularizes less effectively because the signal has already been contracted back to d_model

Apply LoRA to W1 and W2 simultaneously when fine-tuning: targeting only one matrix underutilizes LoRA's capacity; the two matrices form a coupled bottleneck, and low-rank updates to both capture more expressive adaptations

Initialize the output projection W2 with smaller variance in deep networks: the GPT-2 paper proposed initializing the last projection in each sublayer with variance scaled by 1/sqrt(2L) where L is number of layers, preventing excessive activation magnitude growth at initialization
Performance & scalability
The compute for both FFN matrix multiplications over a batch of
sequences each of length
is:

For Llama-3-8B at sequence length 4096 and batch 1: approximately 965 GFLOP per layer. Across 32 layers, the FFN alone consumes roughly 30 TFLOP per forward pass. At decode time with batch size 1, this is dominated not by FLOP throughput but by weight transfer bandwidth.

At batch size 1, FFN compute is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound; throughput improves with larger batches that amortize weight loading cost across more tokens simultaneously
Fusing the two linear layers and activation into one CUDA kernel (Flash FFN) reduces HBM reads/writes by keeping the intermediate activation in SRAM; implementations exist in Triton and FlashInfer

Speculative decoding reduces FFN compute by verifying multiple draft tokens per step; the draft model's FFN dominates its total cost, so a small draft model (1-3B) paired with a large target (70B+) gives efficient combined throughput
Tooling & ecosystem
torch.nn.Linear
: the core FFN building block; use
bias=False
and pre-norm for Llama-style architectures
transformers LlamaMLP
: Hugging Face reference implementation of SwiGLU in modeling_llama.py; readable, well-tested, and a good canonical implementation to study
peft (LoRA)
: the
target_modules=["gate_proj","up_proj","down_proj"]
config applies LoRA adapters to all three SwiGLU matrices simultaneously — the recommended approach for instruction fine-tuning Llama models
rome
memit
: model editing libraries that update specific rows/columns of W2 to change factual associations; depend on the key-value memory interpretation of the FFN
TransformerLens
: mechanistic interpretability library with hooks into
blocks.{i}.mlp.W_in
and
blocks.{i}.mlp.W_out
— essential for studying FFN neuron behaviour in GPT-2 and other open models
FlashInfer
and
vLLM
: production inference engines that fuse and quantize FFN layers to minimize memory bandwidth usage at decode time

Security & reliability

Factual hallucination source: incorrect or outdated facts stored in FFN layers cannot be corrected at inference time without fine-tuning or model editing; retrieval augmentation (RAG) is the standard mitigation for knowledge freshness in agent systems

Model editing side-effects: targeted edits to W2 via ROME or MEMIT can inadvertently affect semantically adjacent associations; always evaluate edited models on factual consistency benchmarks beyond the targeted fact

Quantization error accumulation: aggressive 4-bit quantization of FFN weights introduces per-layer noise that accumulates across 32+ layers; use higher precision (8-bit or bfloat16) for the first and last FFN layers, which tend to be most sensitive

Knowledge extraction risk: FFN neurons can memorize and reproduce verbatim training data; models trained on private corpora are at risk of leaking sensitive text through targeted activation probing or extraction attacks

Gradient explosion in FFN at large d_ff: without careful initialization and gradient clipping, the large parameter matrices can produce gradient norms orders of magnitude larger than other components; always clip gradient norms (typically to 1.0) during transformer training

NaN from uninitialized biases: if bias tensors are not properly zeroed before training, FFN outputs can be NaN or Inf on the first step; always verify initialization explicitly in custom implementations
Key takeaways
The FFN is a position-wise two-layer MLP applied identically and independently to every token — it provides per-token nonlinear transformation with no cross-token interaction
The standard expansion ratio is
; SwiGLU variants use approximately
with three matrices to maintain comparable parameter count
In most transformer models, the FFN holds more parameters than the attention sublayer and is the primary storage site for factual knowledge learned during pre-training
Modern best practice: pre-norm (LayerNorm before the FFN), SwiGLU or GeGLU activation, bias-free projection matrices, and residual addition after dropout

FFN compute scales linearly with sequence length unlike attention's quadratic cost, but at batch size 1 it is memory-bandwidth-bound; quantization and larger batch sizes are the main throughput levers

The FFN can be interpreted as a key-value memory: rows of W1 are keys that detect input patterns, columns of W2 are values that emit content; this grounds targeted model editing methods like ROME and MEMIT
Mixture-of-Experts architectures scale FFN capacity by routing each token to a subset of parallel expert FFNs, keeping activated parameters constant while total parameters grow
Mental model

Think of the attention sublayer as a librarian who fetches relevant books from the shelves based on the current query: it combines information from across the context into a rich packet for each token. The FFN is then a specialist expert who reads that packet in isolation and processes it independently, drawing on a vast internal reference library (the W1/W2 weights) to transform the representation, recall associated facts, and produce a refined result. The specialist does not consult other specialists (no cross-token interaction); they simply react to what the librarian assembled, using everything they absorbed during training. Scaling up d_ff is like giving each specialist a larger reference library; switching to Mixture-of-Experts is like hiring eight specialists and routing each query to the most relevant one.
